title: About
text:
I studied psychology at UC Santa Barbara, which is a roundabout way of saying I spent four years learning how people make decisions and then realized I wanted to be the one giving them better data to decide with. A math post-bacc at CUNY filled in the quantitative gaps, and I’ve been working in data ever since.
My first job was operations at ZocDoc, where I learned that most business problems aren’t modeling problems, they’re “nobody is looking at the data” problems. At Square I moved into analytics during the hardware transition from swipe to chip to contactless readers. I spent 5 years there, got promoted from product specialist to data analyst, and built a tool that automated support case labeling and saved the CS org ~400 hours a week.
I joined Atlassian in 2020 and stayed for 6 years. The work I’m proudest of: building the company’s first Ideal Customer Profile and Buying Group models for the enterprise GTM org. Propensity scoring on 500M+ row contact datasets, segmentation that identified accounts 5x more likely to convert, and a contact enrichment strategy that attributed $20M in bookings. I also became the team’s AI person — I got a 30-person data org from “what’s an MCP server?” to running LLM-assisted analyses against Databricks in a few weeks.
In 2017, while I was still at Square, I taught a data analytics course at General Assembly. It was 10 weeks of SQL, statistics, and experimentation design with a room full of career changers. Teaching people who are learning something for the first time is the fastest way to find out whether you actually understand it yourself.
I was laid off from Atlassian in March 2026. Currently in San Francisco, looking for senior data science roles where I can do the kind of work I like best: building things that help teams make better decisions, and figuring out where AI makes that faster.

title: About
text:
Education

B.A. Psychology — UC Santa Barbara
Post-Baccalaureate, Mathematics — City University of New York

title: Job Title Normalizer
text: View on GitHub

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
What This Project Does
CRM databases are full of job titles that look like they were typed by someone in a hurry — because they were. You get “Sr. Dir. Mktg (EMEA)”, “SENIOR DIRECTOR ENGINEERING”, “Snr. Direcotr, Engg” — all describing the same kind of person but completely different as text. When you need to segment contacts by seniority or function — which is the first step in building buying groups, scoring leads, or targeting campaigns — this inconsistency makes everything downstream unreliable.
This project trains a neural network to classify raw, messy titles into two standardized labels simultaneously:

Seniority — one of 10 levels, from Individual Contributor through C-Suite
Function — one of 10 categories: Engineering, Data, Product, Marketing, Sales, Finance, HR, Operations, Design, Legal

The model predicts both at once through a shared representation, which is more efficient and more accurate than building two separate classifiers.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: Why This Matters in the Real World
text:
Why This Matters in the Real World
At Atlassian, I built contact segmentation and buying group analysis for enterprise marketing. The prerequisite to all of that work was normalizing job titles — you can’t identify coverage gaps in a buying group if you don’t know who’s a VP vs. an IC, or who’s in engineering vs. marketing. Title normalization was the critical first step.
This project isolates that step as a standalone system. The seniority and function labels it produces are exactly the features that would feed into the lead scoring and buying group models in a production pipeline.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: The Data: Why CRM Noise Is Harder Than It Looks
text:
The Data: Why CRM Noise Is Harder Than It Looks
Instead of using clean, synthetic data that would make the model look artificially good, the data generator mirrors what real CRM exports actually look like. There are 15 categories of noise, all drawn from real-world data quality issues:
The obvious stuff — abbreviations (Sr., Dir., Mgr.), case variation (UPPERCASE, lowercase, Title Case), extra whitespace, and character-swap typos.
The stuff that actually makes CRM data painful:

Truncation — CRM fields have character limits. “Senior Vice President of Engineering” becomes “Senior Vice Presi…” and now you’re missing the critical information
Geographic qualifiers — Someone appended “(EMEA)” or “- North America” because that’s what their admin system required
Credential suffixes — “MBA”, “CPA”, “PMP” stuck on the end because people copy their LinkedIn bio into the title field
Misspellings — Not just swapped characters, but real mistakes: “Manger” for Manager, “Engineeer” with a triple e, “Marekting” for Marketing
Word reordering — “Director of Engineering” vs “Engineering, Director” — different CRM systems format differently
Encoding artifacts — Smart quotes, em dashes, HTML entities, non-breaking spaces from system imports
Word drops — Lazy data entry where someone typed “VP” instead of “VP of Engineering”

This noise is layered stochastically — each title gets a random subset — so the model has to generalize to combinations it hasn’t seen.

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
The Models: Why Multi-Task Learning

What Multi-Task Learning Is
Instead of training one model for seniority and a separate one for function, we train one model that predicts both simultaneously. The key insight is that seniority and function share structure. “CTO” tells you both seniority (C-Suite) and function (Engineering) at once. “Manager” tells you seniority but not function. By sharing internal representations, the model learns this structure more efficiently.
The architecture has three parts:

Word embeddings — Each token in a title (after tokenization) maps to a 64-dimensional vector. During training, the model learns that “VP”, “V.P.”, and “Vice President” should all have similar vectors
Shared encoder — Processes the embedded title into a fixed-size representation. This is where seniority and function signals merge
Task-specific heads — Two separate linear layers, one for each prediction



Two Encoder Architectures
BiLSTM reads the title as a sequence, left-to-right and right-to-left, maintaining memory of what it’s seen. The bidirectional pass means “VP of Engineering” and “Engineering VP” produce similar representations.
CNN slides filters across the title looking for local patterns — 2-word, 3-word, and 4-word combinations like “VP of”, “Director of Engineering”, or “Sr. Dir.” It’s faster and slightly more accurate here because title classification depends on local n-gram patterns more than long-range dependencies.


The Baseline: Know What Simple Gets You
Before going neural, we trained TF-IDF + Logistic Regression — the simplest reasonable approach. TF-IDF converts titles to numeric vectors by counting weighted word frequencies. Logistic Regression draws a decision boundary. This establishes a performance floor: if the neural network can’t beat this, it’s not worth the complexity.

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
Results



Model
Seniority Acc
Function Acc
Both Correct




CNN (PyTorch)
95.2%
93.0%
89.0%


BiLSTM (PyTorch)
94.5%
92.6%
88.0%


TF-IDF + LogReg (baseline)
93.3%
93.3%
87.6%



Trained on 16,000 synthetic titles, evaluated on 4,000 held-out.
Why these numbers are realistic, not inflated: The accuracy is in the high 80s to mid 90s — not 99%. That’s by design. The synthetic noise is calibrated to be aggressive, and the model genuinely struggles with ambiguous cases. “Senior” vs “Lead” seniority is hard when the title is truncated. “Operations” vs “Engineering” function is hard when the title says “DevOps Manager” (both are reasonable). Real CRM data would likely push combined accuracy down another 5-10 points.
Where the neural models earn their keep: The gap over TF-IDF (89% vs 87.6% combined) is concentrated in the hardest cases — abbreviated titles, misspellings, and reordered titles where word-level embeddings capture similarity that bag-of-words approaches miss.

Per-Class Performance
The model performs best on classes with distinctive vocabulary:

C-Suite — near-perfect (CTO, CFO, CMO are unambiguous)
Legal — distinctive terms (Counsel, Paralegal, Compliance)
Design — UX/UI vocabulary is specific

It struggles most where vocabulary overlaps:

Operations vs Engineering — “DevOps” sits at the boundary
Individual Contributor vs Senior — when seniority markers get truncated or misspelled
HR vs Operations — “People Operations” is an HR function despite the word “Operations”

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
Training: How the Model Learns

Early Stopping
The model trains for up to 30 passes through the data (epochs). After each pass, we check performance on held-out data. If performance stops improving for 5 consecutive epochs (patience), training halts and we revert to the best checkpoint. This prevents the model from memorizing the training data — a problem called overfitting that’s especially dangerous with 20,000 samples.


What the Model Learns
During training, the embedding layer discovers semantic structure: abbreviations cluster with their full forms, seniority markers group together, function-specific terms form distinct neighborhoods. This learned vocabulary is the main advantage over TF-IDF, which treats every distinct string as a completely independent feature.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: How This Connects to the Lead Scoring Pipeline
text:
How This Connects to the Lead Scoring Pipeline
Title normalization is Step 1 in a contact data pipeline:

Normalize titles → standardized seniority and function labels (this project)
Build contact features → VP+ count, function diversity, senior density per account
Score accounts → propensity model using contact features + firmographics
Identify gaps → buying group completeness analysis

Without reliable title normalization, everything downstream is built on noise. If “Sr. Dir. Mktg” doesn’t get classified as a senior director in marketing, the buying group analysis doesn’t know there’s a senior marketing leader on the account, and the gap analysis might incorrectly recommend finding one.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production

Real labeled data — Retrain on titles from actual CRM exports (Salesforce, HubSpot). The synthetic generator gets you 80% of the way, but production titles have patterns no generator anticipates
Active learning — Flag low-confidence predictions for human review. A model that says “60% engineering, 35% data” is asking for help — route those to a human and use the corrections to retrain
Fine-tuned LLM — For the hardest 10% of titles (creative titles, non-English, multi-role), a fine-tuned language model would capture context that word embeddings miss
Monitoring — Track prediction confidence distributions over time. If a new client uses unusual title conventions, confidence will drop and you’ll know to retrain

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
Stack
Python | PyTorch | scikit-learn | Pandas | NumPy | Matplotlib | pytest

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text: View on GitHub

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
The Problem
Data teams answer the same business questions differently depending on who’s asking. A VP of Sales wants pipeline forecasts with revenue implications. A product manager wants user engagement metrics with cohort breakdowns. The underlying data is the same — the context is what changes.
Adaptive Analyst Agent is an AI-powered data analyst that automatically adapts its analysis style, metric selection, and communication approach based on who it’s talking to.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
How It Works
The agent uses Claude’s tool use capabilities to:

Load stakeholder context — Markdown profiles defining each audience’s priorities, KPIs, and communication preferences
Query data — Execute SQL against a DuckDB database with synthetic GTM data
Adapt the analysis — Frame results, choose visualizations, and adjust technical depth based on the active stakeholder context


Architecture

Agent loop — Claude API with tool use for multi-step reasoning
Context system — Configurable markdown files (stakeholder profiles, domain knowledge, analysis frameworks)
Execution layer — Sandboxed SQL and Python execution via DuckDB
UI — Streamlit app for interactive exploration

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
In Action
Same question, different stakeholder — the agent adapts automatically:

Executive asks “How is pipeline trending?” → Board-level summary: total pipeline value, LTV/CAC ratio, YoY comparisons, and a one-line assessment of whether the quarter is on track.
Marketing asks “How is pipeline trending?” → Campaign-level breakdown: channel attribution, cost per MQL by source, specific winners and losers, and recommendations for budget reallocation.

The underlying SQL queries overlap significantly. What changes is metric selection, aggregation level, and how the results are framed.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
Key Design Choices

Tool use over RAG — Structured data questions benefit from live SQL queries, not pre-embedded answers. The agent needs to act (run queries, generate charts), not just retrieve static content.
Read-only database as primary security layer — The agent executes arbitrary SQL, so the database connection is read-only by design. No risk of accidental writes or deletions regardless of what the LLM generates.
Full context loading vs. selective retrieval — Stakeholder profiles and domain knowledge are loaded entirely into the prompt rather than retrieved via similarity search. This is pragmatic for the current scale (a handful of profiles, a few hundred lines of domain context) and avoids the retrieval failures that plague RAG on small corpora.
DuckDB over SQLite — Columnar storage is better suited for analytical queries. Supports window functions and CTEs natively.
Markdown for context — Easier to read, version, and customize than YAML or JSON. Stakeholder profiles read like documentation.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
Stack
Python | Claude API | DuckDB | Streamlit | Pandas | Plotly

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text: View on GitHub

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
What This Project Does
Imagine you’re running marketing at a B2B software company. You have 1,500 accounts in your pipeline — companies that might buy your product. But your team can only actively engage with maybe 200 of them this quarter. How do you decide which 200?
This project builds two tools to answer that question:

A propensity model that scores every account from 0 to 100% based on how likely they are to close a deal
A buying group analyzer that looks at accounts that should be closing but aren’t, figures out what’s missing (the right people aren’t involved), and recommends exactly who marketing should go find

Together, they turn a chaotic spreadsheet of accounts into a prioritized action plan.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: Why This Matters in the Real World
text:
Why This Matters in the Real World
At Atlassian, I did exactly this work — analyzing which contact profiles showed up on deals that closed, identifying accounts with coverage gaps, and building enrichment strategies. That analysis identified $50M in estimated pipeline from contact addition strategies.
This project recreates that methodology on synthetic data so I can walk through the full approach openly, from data generation to model evaluation to strategic recommendations.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: The Data: What a CRM Looks Like
text:
The Data: What a CRM Looks Like
Before we get into the model, it helps to understand what data B2B marketing teams actually work with. The project generates synthetic data across four tables that mirror what you’d find in a real CRM (Customer Relationship Management) system like Salesforce:

Accounts (1,500 rows)
Each row is a company. We know their industry (Technology, Healthcare, etc.), size (employee count, annual revenue), segment (SMB, Mid-Market, or Enterprise), and tech stack — which software tools they already use. We also know if they’re already a customer of ours.


Contacts (10,504 rows)
Each row is a person at one of those companies. We know their job title, job function (Engineering, Marketing, Sales, etc.), seniority (from Individual Contributor up to C-Suite), and how they entered our system (paid search, organic, events, etc.).


Opportunities (800 rows)
Each row is a potential deal. It’s tied to an account and has a stage (Discovery → Evaluation → Proposal → Negotiation → Closed Won or Closed Lost), a dollar amount, and an outcome — did we win or lose?


Contact-Opportunity Bridge (1,944 rows)
This is the crucial table. It tells us which people are involved in which deals, and what role they play: Champion (our internal advocate), Decision Maker (signs the check), Evaluator (tests the product), Influencer (shapes opinion), or Blocker (raises objections).

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: Feature Engineering: Turning Raw Data Into Signals
text:
Feature Engineering: Turning Raw Data Into Signals
Raw CRM data isn’t directly useful for a model. A company name or a job title string doesn’t mean anything mathematically. Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into numeric signals that a model can learn from.
We build features at three levels, from weakest to strongest:

Level 1: Firmographic & Technographic Features
“What kind of company is this?”

Segment encoding — We convert “SMB / Mid-Market / Enterprise” into three separate yes/no columns. Why not just use 1/2/3? Because the relationship isn’t linear — Mid-Market actually has the highest win rate, not Enterprise. Separate columns let the model learn each segment’s unique effect.
Log-transformed size — Employee count and revenue are heavily skewed (most companies are small, a few are enormous). A log transform compresses this range so the model doesn’t treat a 20,000-person company as 400x more important than a 50-person one. The insight is that going from 50 to 500 employees matters more than going from 5,000 to 5,500.
Tech stack signals — We parse the comma-separated list of tools and create two features: a count of “high-signal” tools (Jira, Salesforce, Slack — tools that suggest the company is in our ecosystem), plus individual flags for the top 10 tools.



Level 2: Contact Composition Features
“Who do we know at this company?”
This is where it gets interesting. We aggregate everyone we know at an account:

VP+ count — How many VP or C-Suite contacts do we have? Seniority correlates strongly with deal closure.
Function diversity — Are we talking to one department or five? Deals that involve multiple departments are harder to start but more likely to close.
Senior density — What fraction of our contacts are senior? An account where 3 of 10 contacts are VPs is different from one where 1 of 50 is.
Technical + Business mix — Do we have contacts from both the “builder” side (Engineering, IT, Product) AND the “buyer” side (Marketing, Sales, Finance)? Deals close when both sides are at the table.



Level 3: Buying Group Features
“Who’s actively involved in the sales process?”
The strongest signals come from the deal contact data:

Deal contact count — How many distinct people are engaged on active deals?
Role coverage — Of the four key roles (Champion, Decision Maker, Evaluator, Influencer), how many are present?
Buying group completeness — A composite 0-100 score combining role coverage, seniority presence, function diversity, and technical/business mix

In total, we generate 49 features from these three levels.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
The Model: Logistic Regression

What Logistic Regression Does
At its core, logistic regression answers a yes/no question: will this account close a deal? It takes all 49 features as inputs and outputs a probability between 0% and 100%.
Think of it like a weighted scorecard. Each feature gets a weight (called a coefficient). Positive weights increase the score; negative weights decrease it. The model learns these weights from historical data — it looks at hundreds of accounts that won or lost, and figures out which features are most predictive.
For example, if the model learns that “VP+ contacts on the deal” has a coefficient of +0.10, it means that having senior contacts engaged makes the account meaningfully more likely to close.


Why Logistic Regression Over Fancier Models
We also trained a Random Forest — a more complex model that builds hundreds of decision trees and averages their predictions. It performed slightly better by some measures, but we chose logistic regression because:

Interpretability: You can look at the coefficients and say “VP+ engagement is our strongest signal, tech stack is second.” Try explaining a 100-tree ensemble to a marketing VP.
It’s what production systems use: Marketing platforms like Marketo and HubSpot use logistic regression under the hood for lead scoring. Building with the same approach makes the work directly transferable.
The performance gap was tiny: The fancier model wasn’t enough better to justify the complexity.



What is Regularization?
With 49 features and only ~580 accounts, there’s a risk of overfitting — the model memorizes quirks of the training data instead of learning real patterns. Regularization is a technique that penalizes large coefficients, essentially telling the model: “don’t put too much weight on any single feature.”
We use L2 regularization (also called Ridge), controlled by a parameter called C. Lower C = stronger regularization = simpler model. We tested C values of 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, and 10.0, and the model performed best at C=0.01 — fairly strong regularization, which makes sense given our relatively small dataset.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: How We Evaluate: Business Metrics That Matter
text:
How We Evaluate: Business Metrics That Matter
Technical metrics like accuracy aren’t very useful here. If 64% of accounts lose, a model that always predicts “lose” would be 64% accurate but completely useless. Instead, we evaluate with metrics that answer real business questions.

AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve)
What it is: A number between 0 and 1 that measures how well the model separates winners from losers. An AUC of 0.5 means the model is no better than flipping a coin. An AUC of 1.0 means perfect separation.
Our result: 0.608 on the held-out test set.
What that means: The model is meaningfully better than random. In practice, AUC values of 0.60-0.70 are common for account-level propensity models — the signal exists but it’s noisy, because many factors that determine whether a deal closes (budget timing, competitive dynamics, internal politics) aren’t in the data.


Precision @ K
What it is: If you only look at the top K% of model-scored accounts, what fraction actually won?
Our results:








Top K%
Precision
What it means




Top 10%
54.5%
More than half of the highest-scored accounts actually won


Top 20%
34.8%
About a third — still above the 36% baseline


Top 30%
41.2%
Concentrated wins in the top tier



Why it matters: This is the metric marketing cares about most. “If I can only email 100 accounts this quarter, which ones?” The model says: pick the top-scored ones, and you’ll hit winners 55% of the time instead of 36%.


Lift Chart
What it is: We divide all accounts into 10 equal groups (deciles) based on their score, from highest to lowest. For each group, we measure how much more likely they are to win compared to the overall average. This ratio is called lift.
Our result: The top decile converts at 1.4x the baseline rate. Accounts in the bottom deciles convert well below baseline.
Why it matters: Lift tells you the concentration of value. If the top 30% of scored accounts capture a disproportionate share of wins, you can focus your team’s time there and ignore the rest.


Revenue Capture Curve
What it is: If you work through accounts from highest-scored to lowest, what percentage of total won revenue have you captured at each point?
Why it matters: It answers “how much money am I leaving on the table?” If the top 30% of scored accounts capture 50% of won revenue, the model is doing real work. If it’s close to 30% (what random selection would give you), the model isn’t helping.


Calibration
What it is: Are the predicted probabilities trustworthy? If the model says an account has a 40% chance of closing, does it actually close about 40% of the time?
Why it matters: If you tell a sales team “this account has a 50% chance of closing,” that number needs to be real. A poorly calibrated model might assign 50% to accounts that actually close 20% of the time, which erodes trust.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: Feature Importance: What Drives the Score
text:
Feature Importance: What Drives the Score
The beauty of logistic regression is that you can look directly at the coefficients to see what matters:
Top positive drivers (increase propensity):

VP+ engagement on deals — The single strongest signal. Having senior contacts actively involved in the sales process is the best predictor of a win.
Complementary tech stack — Accounts that already use tools in our ecosystem (HubSpot, Slack) are more likely to buy.
VP+ contact count — Even beyond the deal level, just knowing senior people at an account is predictive.

Top negative drivers (decrease propensity):

SMB segment — Smaller companies close at lower rates, likely due to smaller budgets and less formalized purchasing.

Both the logistic regression and random forest models agreed on the top signals, which is a good sign — it means the patterns are robust, not artifacts of one particular model type.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
Buying Group Analysis

What Is a Buying Group?
In B2B sales, purchasing decisions are rarely made by one person. A typical enterprise deal involves 4-7 people playing different roles:

Champion — Your internal advocate who pushes for the deal
Decision Maker — The person who signs the check (usually a VP or C-Suite exec)
Evaluator — The person who tests the product and assesses technical fit
Influencer — Someone whose opinion shapes the decision (could be a respected engineer, a trusted advisor)
Blocker — Someone who raises objections or slows the process

A deal is much more likely to close when all four positive roles are filled. If you’re missing a Champion, nobody’s pushing internally. If you’re missing a Decision Maker, there’s no one to sign off.


Completeness Scoring
We score each account’s buying group on a 0-100 scale across four dimensions (25 points each):








Dimension
What it measures
Full marks if…




Role coverage
Key buying roles present
All 4 roles filled


Seniority mix
Exec + mid-level engagement
Both VP+ and Director/Manager


Function diversity
Multiple departments involved
3+ distinct functions


Tech + Business
Builders and buyers both involved
Technical AND business contacts





The Results: Completeness Predicts Wins



Completeness Tier
Win Rate




Complete (75-100)
51%


High (50-75)
38%


Medium (25-50)
27%


Low (0-25)
24%



Accounts with complete buying groups win at 2x the rate of those with low completeness. This isn’t surprising — it’s hard to close a deal when the right people aren’t at the table — but the magnitude of the difference gives marketing a clear mandate: invest in filling coverage gaps.


Gap Analysis: What’s Missing and What to Do About It
The analysis identified 464 accounts with specific coverage gaps. The most common gaps:

Missing buying roles (Champions and Decision Makers are the most commonly absent)
No VP+ contact engaged on the deal
Only one department represented (usually the team that initiated the conversation)

For each gap account, the system generates a specific enrichment recommendation: “This account needs a Champion in Engineering” or “No VP+ contact — target Director or VP-level outreach.”


Estimating Pipeline Value
The final step is quantifying the opportunity. If we assume that filling coverage gaps increases win rate by 10 percentage points (conservative, given the tier differences above), the gap accounts represent an estimated $286M in addressable pipeline from enrichment.
This is the number that gets executive attention. It translates the analysis from “interesting data science project” into “here’s why you should fund this enrichment program.”

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
The Targeting Framework
The most powerful view combines both layers — propensity scores and buying group completeness — into a 2×2 framework:









Complete Buying Group
Gaps Exist




High Propensity
Close now — sales should be all over these
Enrich & close — highest-ROI enrichment targets


Low Propensity
Nurture — engaged but signals are weak
Low priority — gaps AND weak signals



The upper-right quadrant is the sweet spot: accounts the model thinks are likely to buy, but where the buying group isn’t complete. These are the accounts where a targeted contact addition — finding the missing Champion, engaging the VP — could tip the deal.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production
This project uses synthetic data and runs offline. In a production environment, several things would change:

Title normalization — Real job titles are messy (“Sr. Dir. of Eng” vs “Senior Director, Engineering”). I’d use fuzzy matching or an LLM to standardize before feature extraction.
Temporal validation — Instead of random train/test splits, I’d train on earlier time periods and test on later ones. This better simulates how the model would perform on genuinely unseen future data.
Live scoring pipeline — Integrate with Salesforce or HubSpot via scheduled batch scoring, so scores update automatically as new contacts and deal activity come in.
Model monitoring — Track AUC and calibration over time, with alerts when performance drops. Retrain quarterly or when the product/market changes significantly.
A/B test the model — Before full rollout, randomly assign accounts to model-scored vs. control groups and measure the incremental lift in pipeline and bookings. This is the gold standard for proving the model actually drives results, not just correlates with them.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
Stack
Python | Pandas | scikit-learn | Matplotlib | Plotly | Streamlit | Jupyter

title: AI Adoption Playbook for Data Science Teams
text:
Most data science teams know AI is important but don’t know where to start. They’ve used ChatGPT for quick questions or their company’s internal LLM product, but they haven’t integrated AI into their actual workflows — the daily work of writing queries, analyzing data, documenting findings, and collaborating with stakeholders.
At Atlassian, I led AI adoption across a 30+ person data org. Most of the team’s only AI experience was using Rovo, Atlassian’s internal LLM product. Within weeks, team members were running natural language queries against our Databricks data warehouse, having LLMs write analysis summaries directly to Confluence, and using standardized knowledge files that encoded our team’s best practices and table documentation.
This playbook captures the framework I used — what worked, what blocked adoption, and how to sequence it so a team goes from zero to productive.

title: AI Adoption Playbook for Data Science Teams
text:
Most data science teams know AI is important but don’t know where to start. They’ve used ChatGPT for quick questions or their company’s internal LLM product, but they haven’t integrated AI into their actual workflows — the daily work of writing queries, analyzing data, documenting findings, and collaborating with stakeholders.
At Atlassian, I led AI adoption across a 30+ person data org. Most of the team’s only AI experience was using Rovo, Atlassian’s internal LLM product. Within weeks, team members were running natural language queries against our Databricks data warehouse, having LLMs write analysis summaries directly to Confluence, and using standardized knowledge files that encoded our team’s best practices and table documentation.
This playbook captures the framework I used — what worked, what blocked adoption, and how to sequence it so a team goes from zero to productive.

title: AI Adoption Playbook for Data Science Teams
text:
The Framework
AI adoption for a DS team isn’t a training session. It’s a sequence of unlocks, where each step builds on the last. Skip a step and people get stuck. Get the order right and each unlock makes the next one obvious.

Step 1: Get People Into the Terminal
The problem you’re solving: Most data scientists live in notebooks and GUIs. They’ve never run an LLM from the command line, installed a CLI tool, or configured an MCP server. This is the single biggest blocker — not skepticism about AI, but unfamiliarity with the environment where AI tools actually work.
What to do:

Walk people through terminal basics if needed — many data scientists are strong in Python and SQL but have never configured a CLI tool
Install an AI-enabled code editor (Cursor, VS Code with extensions, or a CLI-based LLM tool)
Get them to run their first LLM interaction from the terminal, not a web browser
Make it hands-on — do this in 1:1 sessions, not a slide deck. The “aha moment” comes from seeing it work on their own machine

Why this matters: Every subsequent step depends on being comfortable running AI tools locally. If someone can’t get past terminal setup, nothing else in this playbook works.
Common blocker: Time. People know they should learn this but can’t carve out the hours. The solution is to make it feel small — a 30-minute 1:1 where you set everything up together. Once they see the capabilities, they find the time on their own.


Step 2: Connect the LLM to Internal Tools via MCP
The problem you’re solving: A standalone LLM can answer questions and write code, but it can’t interact with your company’s systems. MCP (Model Context Protocol) connections turn the LLM from a chatbot into a tool that can read from and write to the systems your team actually uses.
What to connect:

Documentation tools (Confluence, Notion, Google Docs) — the LLM can read existing docs for context and write analysis summaries directly
Project management (Jira, Linear, Asana) — the LLM can create tickets, update status, and pull context about what the team is working on
Code repositories (Bitbucket, GitHub) — the LLM can read the codebase, understand your data models, and write code that follows your team’s patterns
Data platforms (Databricks CLI, Snowflake CLI) — the LLM can query your data warehouse using natural language and run analysis on the results

The unlock: Once the LLM is connected to Confluence and your data warehouse, a data scientist can say “query the marketing attribution table for Q4 campaign performance, analyze the results, and write a summary to Confluence.” That workflow — which normally takes hours of writing SQL, interpreting results, and formatting a doc — becomes a conversation.
How to do it:

Set up MCP servers for each tool your team uses
Test each connection yourself first — work out the gotchas before asking the team to try
Start with the tools people use daily (documentation and code repos before project management)
Create a simple install guide with copy-paste commands — remove every possible friction point



Step 3: Build Knowledge Files That Encode Team Expertise
The problem you’re solving: Every data science team has tribal knowledge — which tables to use, which joins are tricky, what the column names actually mean, which metrics are the “real” ones. This knowledge lives in people’s heads, scattered Confluence pages, and Slack threads. New team members spend months learning it. LLMs don’t have it at all.
What to build:

Table documentation — which tables exist, what they contain, key columns, common joins, and known gotchas (“this table has duplicates on account_id if you don’t filter by is_current”)
Nomenclature and definitions — what your team means by “MQL,” “pipeline,” “closed-won,” segment names, and metric definitions
Query patterns — common SQL patterns your team uses, including CTEs, window functions, and filters that are always needed
Best practices — how your team structures analyses, naming conventions, how to document findings
Visualization standards — a preamble that enforces your company’s brand guidelines (colors, fonts, chart styles) so every LLM-generated visualization is presentation-ready. We standardized on Plotly with a preamble that aligned to Atlassian’s viz requirements — every chart the LLM produced looked like it came from the team, not from a chatbot

How to format them: Markdown files that the LLM loads as context. Keep them concise, specific, and up to date. A good knowledge file reads like a cheat sheet a senior team member would write for a new hire.
The unlock: When a team member asks the LLM to query a table, the LLM already knows the join conditions, the gotchas, and the right metric definitions. The analysis comes back correct the first time instead of requiring three rounds of debugging.


Step 4: Create Standardized Skills and Workflows
The problem you’re solving: Once people have MCP connections and knowledge files, they’ll start building their own workflows. Some will be great. Many will reinvent the wheel. Without standardization, you get 40 people doing the same thing 40 different ways.
What to standardize:

Analysis workflow — a standard pattern for how to use the LLM to run a data analysis: query the data, validate the results, generate visualizations, write a summary
Documentation workflow — how to have the LLM write findings to Confluence in a consistent format
Code review workflow — how to use the LLM to review SQL and Python against team standards
Onboarding workflow — how a new team member sets up their AI tools on day one

How to distribute: Build these as skills or templates that people can reference or invoke. Share them in a central location (a Confluence page, a repo, a shared config file). Make them easy to copy and modify.


Step 5: Measure and Iterate
The problem you’re solving: “Are people actually using this?” and “Is it making us more productive?” are hard to answer without intentional measurement.
What to track:

Adoption breadth — how many team members have completed setup and are actively using AI tools
Use case coverage — which workflows are people using AI for, and which are they still doing manually
Quality signals — are LLM-assisted analyses catching errors that manual work would miss? Are summaries being accepted or rewritten?
Qualitative feedback — regular check-ins to understand what’s working and what’s frustrating

What not to do: Don’t try to measure “time saved” precisely. It’s nearly impossible to A/B test individual productivity, and the numbers will be challenged. Instead, track adoption and collect stories — “I used to spend 2 hours writing this Confluence summary, now I describe what I want and review the output in 15 minutes.”

title: AI Adoption Playbook for Data Science Teams
text:
Common Pitfalls

“Let’s do a training session”
Group training sessions are useful for awareness but don’t drive adoption. The actual unlock happens in 1:1 sessions where you sit with someone, set up their tools, and run through a workflow on their real data. Budget time for this — it’s the highest-leverage activity.


Starting with the wrong use case
Don’t start with the most complex workflow. Start with the most repetitive one — the thing everyone does weekly that’s tedious but not risky. Documentation, data validation, and exploratory queries are good first use cases. Production model training is not.


Underestimating terminal unfamiliarity
Data scientists who work exclusively in Jupyter notebooks and Tableau may have never configured a CLI tool. Don’t assume this is trivial. Walk people through it.


No knowledge files
Without knowledge files, the LLM will write plausible-looking queries against the wrong tables with the wrong join conditions. The output looks professional but is wrong. Knowledge files are the difference between an LLM that’s useful and one that’s dangerous.


Trusting LLM output without QA
The LLM will write SQL that looks correct but joins on the wrong key. It will generate analysis that cites a metric definition it made up. It will produce a chart with the right format but the wrong data. Knowledge files reduce this significantly — accurate table documentation and query patterns mean fewer hallucinations — but they don’t eliminate it. A data scientist still needs to review every query, validate the numbers, and sanity-check the conclusions. The LLM is a force multiplier, not a replacement for judgment. We never reached a point where output could be trusted blindly, and any adoption framework that promises otherwise is selling something.


Trying to measure ROI too early
AI adoption is an infrastructure investment, not a feature launch. The ROI comes from compounding efficiency gains over months, not from a single measurable event. Track adoption and let the stories accumulate.

title: AI Adoption Playbook for Data Science Teams
section: What I’d Do Differently Next Time
text:
What I’d Do Differently Next Time

Start with MCP connections earlier. Terminal setup + MCP connections should happen in the same session. The MCP connections are what make the whole thing feel magical — without them, it’s just another chatbot.
Build knowledge files before training anyone. Having accurate table documentation and query patterns ready before the first 1:1 means the team’s first experience with AI tools produces correct results, which builds trust.
Create a buddy system. Pair early adopters with skeptics. Peer enablement scales better than one person doing every 1:1.
Record the 1:1 sessions. The same questions come up every time. A library of short walkthroughs would have saved significant time.

title: AI Adoption Playbook for Data Science Teams
text:
Stack

title: Philip Felix
text:
I make marketing teams smarter about who they’re going after and whether it’s working. Targeting, segmentation, reporting that people actually use, and the causal analysis to prove what’s driving revenue vs. what’s just correlated with it. Most recently at Atlassian, where I spent 6 years in the GTM data science org — started on Events analytics during the shift to remote, then moved into targeting, contact health, and the ICP/Buying Group work that drove $20M in bookings and surfaced $50M in recoverable pipeline.
I also became the person who got our 30-person data org actually using AI. Not a slide deck about AI strategy. Hands-on: terminal setup, MCP connections, knowledge files, 1:1 sessions until people stopped asking me for help and started building their own workflows.
Before Atlassian, I was at Square during the hardware transition years (swipe → chip → contactless), and before that I was doing ops at ZocDoc. I have a psychology degree, a math post-bacc, and a teaching stint at General Assembly that permanently ruined my ability to write jargon without flinching.

title: Philip Felix
text:
Featured Projects

Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
You have 1,500 accounts and can only engage 200 this quarter. Which 200? This project builds a propensity model to answer that, plus a buying group analyzer that figures out why good accounts aren’t closing and who’s missing from the deal.
Python, Pandas, scikit-learn, Streamlit


Job Title Normalizer
CRM job titles are a mess. “Sr. Dir. Mktg (EMEA)” and “Senior Director of Marketing” are the same person. This multi-task PyTorch classifier standardizes them into seniority levels and job functions, handling 15 categories of real-world CRM noise that break rule-based approaches.
Python, PyTorch, scikit-learn


AI Adoption Playbook
How I took a 30-person data org from “what’s an MCP server?” to running LLM-assisted analyses in weeks. Not theory. The actual steps, in order, with the blockers I hit and what I’d do differently.
Claude, MCP, Cursor, Databricks CLI


Adaptive Analyst Agent
An LLM-powered analyst that answers the same business question differently depending on who’s asking. A VP gets a board-level summary. A campaign manager gets channel attribution and budget recommendations. Same data, different context.
Python, Claude API, DuckDB, Streamlit
